title: Textos — apropiate de uno y exponelo
goals: Elegí un texto, estudialo a fondo y aprendé a hablar de él: explicarlo, exponerlo, responder preguntas e improvisar.
Cada texto es una parte completa: leelo, aprendé su vocabulario, respondé sobre él y preparate para exponerlo.

aspects.title: Texto 2 — What is a square?
aspects.summary: Apropiate del texto de Hannah Darken hasta poder exponerlo y responder sobre él.

aspects.title: Texto 1 — Why 2 Is the Best Number
aspects.summary: Apropiate de la entrevista a Melanie Matchett Wood hasta poder comentarla y exponerla.

aspects.title: Texto 3 — Do Students Need Four Years of High School Math?
aspects.summary: Apropiate del debate sobre cuánta matemática hace falta hasta poder exponerlo y opinar.

aspects.title: Texto 4 — Should people learn maths until age 18?
aspects.summary: Apropiate del argumento a favor de estudiar matemática hasta los 18 para exponerlo y debatirlo.

source.note: Texto reproducido del cuadernillo. Los epígrafes de las figuras (salvo el del cuadrado, que es del original) son nuestros, descriptivos.

atoms.title: What is a square?
atoms.speaker: narrator

blocks.text: A square is a shape with four straight sides of equal length and four angles of 90 degrees. On the flat surface we use in school, called the Euclidean plane, this definition is strict: any closed figure with equal sides and right angles must have four sides.

image.alt: A blue square with small red marks in its four corners showing the four 90-degree angles.

blocks.caption: An honest square: it has four straight sides of equal length and four 90 degree angles.

blocks.text: But what happens if we change the surface? Geometry can also be studied on curved spaces, and then surprising things occur.
Mathematician Carl Friedrich Gauss introduced the idea of Gaussian curvature to measure how bent a surface is.

items: A plane has zero curvature: it is flat.
A sphere has positive curvature: it curves outward in all directions.
A saddle has negative curvature: it curves down in one direction and up in another.

blocks.text: On a sphere, the curvature is constant and positive. On a saddle, the curvature is negative and changes depending on where you sit.

image.alt: A saddle-shaped surface that curves up in one direction and down in the other, with two lines crossing on it.

blocks.caption: A saddle curves up one way and down the other: this is negative curvature.

blocks.text: Imagine the Earth with its equator and North pole. Walk along a line of longitude from the equator to the pole, then turn 90 degrees and walk down another longitude back to the equator. Finally, walk along the equator to your starting point.
You have created a triangle with three equal sides and three right angles! On the sphere, the angles of a triangle can add up to more than 180 degrees. In this case, they add up to 270.

image.alt: A blue sphere with a triangle drawn on it, formed by two lines of longitude and the equator, meeting at right angles.

blocks.caption: A triangle on a sphere can have three right angles that add up to 270 degrees.

blocks.text: Even stranger, you can draw a two-sided figure called a digon. It is formed by two half great circles meeting at right angles.

The pseudosphere is a surface with constant negative curvature. On it, you can draw shapes with equal sides and right angles that have more than four sides. For example, a five-sided figure with all sides equal and all angles 90 degrees.

image.alt: A funnel-shaped pseudosphere surface with a red five-sided figure drawn on it.

blocks.caption: On a pseudosphere, a shape with equal sides and right angles can have more than four sides.

blocks.text: Although the sides look curved to our eyes, they are actually straight in the sense of geodesics—the shortest paths on the surface.

So, if you thought that equal sides and right angles always define a square, think again. On curved surfaces, you can have triangles, digons, or pentagons with those properties. It all depends on the curvature of the space.

atoms.afterReadingTask: Find 5 example sentences which use the Verb To Be in the text.

atoms.text: A square is a shape with four straight sides of equal length and four angles of 90 degrees.
atoms.gloss: Un cuadrado es una figura de cuatro lados rectos e iguales y cuatro ángulos de 90 grados.
atoms.speaker: narrator
atoms.ipa: ɐ skwˈɛɹ ɪz ɐ ʃˈeɪp wɪð fˈoːɹ stɹˈeɪt sˈaɪdz ʌv ˈiːkwəl lˈɛŋθ ænd fˈoːɹ ˈæŋɡəlz ʌv nˈaɪnti dᵻɡɹˈiːz

atoms.text: On a sphere, the curvature is constant and positive.
atoms.gloss: En una esfera, la curvatura es constante y positiva.

atoms.text: The pseudosphere is a surface with constant negative curvature.
atoms.gloss: La pseudoesfera es una superficie de curvatura negativa constante.
atoms.speaker: narrator
atoms.ipa: ðə sˈuːdoʊsfˌɪɹ ɪz ɐ sˈɜːfɪs wɪð kˈɑːnstənt nˈɛɡətˌɪv kˈɜːvətʃɚ

atoms.text: Although the sides look curved to our eyes, they are actually straight in the sense of geodesics.
atoms.gloss: Aunque los lados parecen curvos a nuestros ojos, en realidad son rectos en el sentido de las geodésicas.

atoms.ipa: ɔːlðˈoʊ ðə sˈaɪdz lˈʊk kˈɜːvd tʊ ˌaʊɚɹ ˈaɪz ðeɪ ɑːɹ ˈæktʃuːəli stɹˈeɪt ɪnðə sˈɛns ʌv dʒˌiːoʊdˈɛsɪks

atoms.text: It is formed by two half great circles meeting at right angles.
atoms.gloss: Está formado por dos semicírculos máximos que se encuentran en ángulo recto.
atoms.speaker: narrator
atoms.ipa: ɪɾ ɪz fˈɔːɹmd baɪ tˈuː hˈæf ɡɹˈeɪt sˈɜːkəlz mˈiːɾɪŋ æt ɹˈaɪt ˈæŋɡəlz

atoms.gloss: Podés dibujar una figura de dos lados llamada dígono.
atoms.speaker: narrator
atoms.ipa: juː kæn dɹˈɔː ɐ tˈuːsˈaɪdᵻd fˈɪɡjɚ kˈɔːld ɐ dˈɪɡɑːn

atoms.gloss: curvatura: cuánto se dobla una superficie
geodésica: el camino más corto sobre una superficie (la 'recta' de un espacio curvo)
esfera: superficie perfectamente redonda, como una pelota
pseudoesfera: superficie de curvatura negativa constante, con forma de trompeta
ángulo recto: un ángulo de 90 grados
dígono: una figura de dos lados (imposible en el plano)

source.note: Pregunta de comprensión nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: What is a square on the Euclidean plane?
promptVariants: How does the text define a square on a flat surface?
replies: A shape with four equal straight sides and four right angles.

answers.text: The text says a square is a shape with four straight sides of equal length and four angles of 90 degrees.
It's a flat shape with four equal sides and four ninety-degree corners.
On the flat Euclidean plane the definition is strict, so only a four-sided figure can be a square.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta de comprensión nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Can a triangle have three right angles?
promptVariants: Is it possible for a triangle to have three ninety-degree angles?
replies: Yes, on a sphere.
Yes, on a sphere a triangle can have three right angles.

answers.text: The text says that on the sphere the angles of a triangle add up to 270, so yes — it can have three right angles.
Yes. On a curved surface the angles of a triangle add up to more than 180 degrees, so all three can be right angles.
This happens because the sphere has positive curvature, the idea of Gaussian curvature the text introduces earlier.
What surprised me is that this triangle looks impossible on paper, but on a globe it is just the equator and two lines of longitude.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta de comprensión nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: What is a digon?
promptVariants: What kind of shape is a digon?
replies: A two-sided figure formed by two half great circles.

answers.text: The text says a digon is a two-sided figure, formed by two half great circles meeting at right angles.
It's a shape with only two sides, made by two half circles on a sphere.
Like the spherical triangle, it is another shape that is impossible on the flat plane but appears once the surface is curved.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta interpretativa nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Why do the sides of these shapes look curved but are actually straight?
promptVariants: The sides look curved. Why does the text say they are really straight?
replies: Because they are geodesics, the shortest paths on the surface.

answers.text: The text says they are actually straight in the sense of geodesics, the shortest paths on the surface.
On a curved surface, straight means the shortest route, and that route looks bent to our eyes.
That is why a triangle on a sphere can have three right angles: its sides are geodesics, not flat lines.
For me this is the key idea of the text: straight depends on the surface, not on how it looks.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta de síntesis nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: What does the whole text want you to rethink?
promptVariants: What is the main point of the text?
replies: That equal sides and right angles do not always mean a square.

answers.text: The conclusion says: if you thought that equal sides and right angles always define a square, think again.
That the definition of a square is not fixed: it depends on the surface you draw it on.
Because curvature changes the rules, you can get triangles, digons or pentagons with equal sides and right angles.
It made me realise that geometry can be very different from what we learn at school.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta de exposición nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: How would you explain Gaussian curvature to a friend?
promptVariants: In simple words, what is Gaussian curvature?
replies: It measures how much a surface bends: zero for a plane, positive for a sphere, negative for a saddle.

answers.text: It's a number that tells you how bent a surface is.
A plane has zero, a sphere is positive and a saddle is negative, and that is what decides which shapes are possible.
I would use a ball and a saddle, or a Pringle chip, to show positive and negative curvature.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Consigna de reformulación nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Explain in your own words what Gaussian curvature is.
atoms.modelAnswer: Gaussian curvature is a way to measure how bent a surface is. A plane has zero curvature because it is flat. A sphere has positive curvature because it curves outward everywhere. A saddle has negative curvature because it curves up one way and down the other.
modelVariants: Gaussian curvature tells us how bent a surface is: it is zero on a plane, positive on a sphere, and negative on a saddle.
scaffold: Say what it measures (how bent a surface is).
Give the three cases: plane, sphere, saddle.
Say what zero, positive and negative curvature mean.

rubric.text: Das los tres casos (plane / sphere / saddle)
Explicás qué significa cada tipo de curvatura

atoms.speaker: student

steps.segment: Gaussian curvature is a way to measure how bent a surface is.

steps.prompt: What is the curvature of a plane?
steps.segment: A plane has zero curvature because it is flat.

steps.prompt: What is the curvature of a sphere?
steps.segment: A sphere has positive curvature because it curves outward everywhere.

steps.prompt: What is the curvature of a saddle?
steps.segment: A saddle has negative curvature because it curves up one way and down the other.

source.note: Consigna de exposición nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Give a one to two minute presentation about the text 'What is a square?'.
atoms.modelAnswer: This text asks a simple question: what is a square? On the flat surface we use at school, the Euclidean plane, a square is a shape with four equal sides and four right angles, and the definition is strict. But if we change the surface, surprising things happen. Gauss measured how bent a surface is with curvature: a plane has zero curvature, a sphere has positive curvature, and a saddle has negative curvature. On a sphere you can draw a triangle with three right angles, and on a pseudosphere a shape with equal sides and right angles can have more than four sides. The sides look curved, but they are actually straight: they are geodesics, the shortest paths on the surface. So the conclusion is that equal sides and right angles do not always mean a square. It all depends on the curvature of the space.
scaffold: Open with the question: what is a square?
On the flat plane: four equal sides and four right angles.
Change the surface: introduce curvature (plane, sphere, saddle).
Give an example: a triangle on a sphere with three right angles.
Key idea: the sides are geodesics — straight means the shortest path.
Conclude: the definition depends on the curvature of the space.

rubric.text: Das la definición en el plano (right angles / sides)
Das un ejemplo en una superficie curva (sphere / pseudosphere)
Concluís que depende de la curvatura del espacio

atoms.speaker: student

steps.prompt: What question does the text ask?
steps.segment: This text asks a simple question: what is a square?

steps.prompt: How is a square defined on the flat Euclidean plane?
steps.segment: On the Euclidean plane, a square is a shape with four equal sides and four right angles, and the definition is strict.
steps.hint: four equal sides, four right angles

steps.prompt: What happens if you change the surface, and what does curvature measure?
steps.segment: But if we change the surface, surprising things happen. Gauss measured how bent a surface is with curvature: a plane has zero, a sphere positive, and a saddle negative.

steps.prompt: Give an example of a strange shape on a curved surface.
steps.segment: On a sphere you can draw a triangle with three right angles, and on a pseudosphere a shape with right angles can have more than four sides.

steps.prompt: Why do the sides look curved but are actually straight?
steps.segment: The sides look curved, but they are actually straight: they are geodesics, the shortest paths on the surface.
So equal sides and right angles do not always mean a square. It all depends on the curvature of the space.

modelVariants: A square usually has four equal sides and four right angles, but only on a flat plane. On a curved surface the rules change: a sphere has positive curvature and a saddle negative. On a sphere a triangle can have three right angles, and on a pseudosphere a shape can have more than four sides. The sides look curved but are really geodesics, the shortest paths. So what counts as a square depends on the curvature of the space.

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: What is the Euclidean plane?
promptVariants: What do we call the flat surface of school geometry?
replies: The flat surface we use in school.

answers.text: The text calls it the flat surface we use in school, where the definition of a square is strict.
It's the ordinary flat surface of school geometry, with no curvature.
It has zero curvature, so on it a square must have exactly four sides.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: How can you draw a triangle with three right angles on the Earth?
promptVariants: Describe the walk that makes a triangle with three right angles.
replies: Walk from the equator to the pole, turn 90 degrees, come back, and follow the equator.

answers.text: The text says to walk along a longitude from the equator to the pole, turn 90 degrees, walk down another longitude, and follow the equator back.
You go from the equator up to the pole, turn a right angle, come back down, and close the triangle along the equator.
I like this one because you can actually trace it on a globe with your finger.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: What is the difference between a plane, a sphere and a saddle?
promptVariants: Compare the curvature of a plane, a sphere and a saddle.
replies: Their curvature: zero, positive and negative.

answers.text: A plane is flat, a sphere curves outward everywhere, and a saddle curves up one way and down the other.
That difference is their Gaussian curvature: zero, positive and negative, and it decides which shapes are possible.
I picture a table, a ball and a Pringle chip to remember the three cases.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Why does the author begin with the school definition of a square?
promptVariants: What is the point of starting from the familiar definition?
replies: To set up a rule that the curved surfaces then break.

answers.text: The strict four-sided definition on the plane is the expectation that the rest of the text overturns with curved surfaces.
She starts from what everyone knows, so the surprising examples feel stronger.
For me it makes the text feel like a small trick: first the obvious, then 'think again'.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Can you give another strange shape from the text?
promptVariants: Besides the spherical triangle, what other shape appears?
replies: A digon, or a five-sided figure with right angles on a pseudosphere.

answers.text: The text mentions a digon, a two-sided figure, and a five-sided figure with equal sides and right angles on a pseudosphere.
Besides the spherical triangle, there is the two-sided digon and a right-angled pentagon on a pseudosphere.
The digon surprised me most: a shape with only two sides sounds impossible.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: What does 'straight' mean on a curved surface?
promptVariants: On a curved surface, what makes a line count as straight?
replies: The shortest path, called a geodesic.

answers.text: The text says the sides are straight in the sense of geodesics, the shortest paths on the surface.
Straight means the shortest route between two points, even if it looks curved to us.
That is why these shapes can have right angles: their sides are geodesics, not flat lines.
This is the single idea I would keep from the whole text.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Do you find this idea useful, or just curious? Why?
promptVariants: What is your opinion of the text's main idea?
replies: Both: it is curious, but it also shows geometry depends on the surface.

answers.text: I think it is surprising, and it also teaches that the rules of geometry change with the surface.
It connects to real things like maps and the Earth, where the surface is a sphere, not a plane.
For me it is mostly beautiful: it shows that 'obvious' rules are only obvious on flat paper.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Cloze nuestro para practicar los tipos de curvatura.

atoms.prompt: Completá con el tipo de curvatura de cada superficie.

items.stem: The curvature of a plane is ___.
The curvature of a sphere is ___.
The curvature of a saddle is ___.
On a sphere, the angles of a triangle add up to ___ degrees.

source.note: Consigna de producción nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Explain the text to a friend who has never studied geometry, in simple words.
atoms.modelAnswer: Imagine a square: four equal sides and four square corners. On flat paper that always means four sides. But if you draw on a ball or another curved surface, the rules change. On a ball you can make a triangle with three square corners, and on a trumpet-shaped surface a shape can have five sides with square corners. The sides look bent, but they are the shortest paths, so they count as straight. So a square is not always a square: it depends on the surface.
scaffold: Start from the everyday square.
Say the flat rule: always four sides.
Change to a ball or a curved surface.
Give one simple example.
End: it depends on the surface.

rubric.text: Cerrás con que depende de la superficie

atoms.speaker: student

steps.prompt: Start from an everyday square: what is it?
steps.segment: Imagine a square: four equal sides and four square corners.

steps.prompt: What is the rule on flat paper?
steps.segment: On flat paper that always means four sides.

steps.prompt: What happens on a curved surface?
steps.segment: But if you draw on a ball or another curved surface, the rules change.
On a ball you can make a triangle with three square corners, and on a trumpet-shaped surface a shape can have five sides with square corners.

steps.prompt: Why are the bent-looking sides straight?
steps.segment: The sides look bent, but they are the shortest paths, so they count as straight.
So a square is not always a square: it depends on the surface.

modelVariants: Think of a square: four equal sides and square corners. On flat paper that means four sides. But on a ball or a curved surface the rules change, so you can get a triangle with three square corners. The sides look bent but they are the shortest paths, so they are really straight. A square isn't always a square: it depends on the surface.

source.note: Consigna de producción nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Choose the sphere and explain what strange shapes appear on it.
atoms.modelAnswer: On a sphere the curvature is positive and constant. Because of that, the angles of a triangle add up to more than 180 degrees, so you can draw a triangle with three right angles. You just walk from the equator to the pole, turn, come back, and follow the equator. You can even draw a digon, a figure with only two sides, formed by two half great circles. On a sphere, straight lines are the shortest paths, so these sides are really straight even if they look curved.
scaffold: Name the sphere's curvature (positive).
Explain the triangle with three right angles.
Describe how to build it (equator, pole).
Add the digon.
Note that the sides are geodesics (shortest paths).

rubric.text: Nombrás la curvatura de la esfera
Explicás el triángulo de ángulos rectos
Aclarás que los lados son geodésicas

atoms.speaker: student

steps.prompt: What is the curvature of a sphere?
steps.segment: On a sphere the curvature is positive and constant.

steps.prompt: What does that do to a triangle?
steps.segment: Because of that, the angles of a triangle add up to more than 180 degrees, so you can draw a triangle with three right angles.

steps.prompt: How do you build that triangle on the Earth?
steps.segment: You just walk from the equator to the pole, turn, come back, and follow the equator.

steps.prompt: What other strange shape can you draw?
steps.segment: You can even draw a digon, a figure with only two sides, formed by two half great circles.

steps.prompt: Why are the sides really straight?
steps.segment: On a sphere, straight lines are the shortest paths, so these sides are really straight even if they look curved.

modelVariants: A sphere has positive curvature, so a triangle there can have three right angles: just walk from the equator to the pole, turn, and come back along the equator. You can even draw a digon, a two-sided figure. The sides are geodesics, so they are straight even if they look curved.

source.note: Consigna de producción nuestra sobre el Texto 2.

atoms.prompt: Give your opinion: does this text change how we should think about geometry?
atoms.modelAnswer: In my opinion, this text changes how we think about geometry. At school we learn that a square always has four sides, and we believe it is a fixed truth. But the text shows that this is only true on a flat plane. On curved surfaces the same rules give triangles, digons or pentagons with equal sides and right angles. So geometry is not one fixed set of rules: it depends on the space. I think this is important because the real world, like the surface of the Earth, is curved, not flat.
scaffold: State your opinion clearly.
Say what we usually believe.
Contrast with what the text shows.
Give the reason (curvature / the space).
Connect to the real world.

rubric.text: Justificás con la curvatura o el espacio

atoms.title: Why 2 Is the Best Number
atoms.speaker: narrator

blocks.text: This text is an interview with Melanie Matchett Wood, a mathematician at Harvard University who recently won a MacArthur Fellowship, also called a genius grant, for her work on open problems in mathematics.

She works in number theory, the study of the whole numbers: 1, 2, 3, and so on. She is fascinated by prime numbers, which are whole numbers greater than 1 that can only be divided by 1 and themselves, such as 2, 3, or 7.

Wood describes her work as inventing methods, not just applying them. In her words: here is a question we have no method to solve, so come up with a method. She compares it to the difference between reading a book and writing a book, which is very different from most people's experience of mathematics at school.

She is drawn to questions about foundational structures, such as the whole numbers, that we do not yet have tools to answer. These structures underpin everything in mathematics, and their difficulty is exactly what excites her.

Her key tools are looking at many concrete examples to see what patterns emerge, bringing in ideas from other areas like probability or geometry, and being willing to try things that do not work and still learn from those failures.
Her favorite number, and favorite prime, is 2. It seems simple, she says, but it is very powerful: 2 is responsible for the concept of even and odd numbers.

She even tells a story. On her university math team the students wore shirts with numbers, and many chose numbers like pi. Hers was 2, and when she graduated they retired her math jersey with the number 2.

sections.heading: What the MacArthur prize means to her

blocks.text: For Wood, the prize is a tremendous honor, especially because the fellowship celebrates creativity. Most people associate creativity with the arts, but she points out that solving math questions no one knows how to answer also requires imagination.

atoms.title: Do Students Need Four Years of High School Math?
atoms.speaker: narrator

blocks.text: This text discusses how much mathematics students should study at school. It compares the systems in the United Kingdom and the United States, after the British prime minister said he wants all students to study maths for longer.

In England, many students stop taking maths after age 16. Students take exams called GCSEs at fifteen or sixteen, and they must pass maths or retake it. After that, they are not required to continue. For university, they take specialized courses called A-Levels in the subjects they want to study.

In contrast, most students in the United States take maths throughout high school. Many states require three maths courses to graduate, and some require four. The traditional sequence is Algebra 1, Geometry and Algebra 2, but many students follow other paths.

Research suggests that students who take more years of maths have better outcomes later, even if they do not choose maths-heavy careers. For some experts this is also an equity issue, because students from disadvantaged backgrounds benefit from stronger maths preparation.

Many countries, including Canada, France, Germany, Finland and Japan, require maths until age 18, but their results vary. The United States scores below the international average on the PISA test, while countries like China and Vietnam score much higher. Experts warn that results depend on many factors, such as teaching methods and social conditions.

The debate reflects a global question: how much maths is enough? More years of study may improve college readiness and confidence, but outcomes also depend on equity, teaching quality and early support.

atoms.prompt: Why does maths open doors to many careers?
promptVariants: How does maths help with careers?
replies: Because it is the language of science, engineering and technology.

answers.text: The text says maths is the language of science, engineering and technology, and it prepares students for any STEM subject.
Because so many jobs in science and technology are built on maths.
It also includes statistics and Big Data, which are needed in medicine, finance and marketing.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Text N°4.

atoms.prompt: How can maths help you in everyday life?
promptVariants: Why is maths useful outside of work?
replies: It helps you make decisions and understand statistics.

answers.text: The text says even people who don't use maths at work make decisions every day about what to buy, who to vote for and how to stay healthy.
It helps you judge claims and numbers so you are not misled.
For me the clearest example is understanding statistics in the news.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Text N°4.

atoms.prompt: Why does the text compare learning maths to learning a language?
promptVariants: What is the language comparison about?
replies: Because both need practice until they become part of your thinking.

answers.text: The text says knowing vocabulary and grammar is not enough; you need to practice until the language becomes part of your thinking.
Like a language, maths takes time and practice, not just memorizing formulas.
That is why studying until 18 matters: it gives time to make maths part of your thinking.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Text N°4.

atoms.prompt: What does the text mean by 'healthy skepticism'?
promptVariants: Why is skepticism a maths skill?
replies: Not accepting ideas without proof.

answers.text: The text says mathematicians don't accept ideas without proof, and this questioning attitude helps us evaluate claims.
It means asking for evidence before believing a claim.
I think this is useful against misleading ads or politicians.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Text N°4.

atoms.prompt: Why does the author mention balancing maths with the arts?
promptVariants: Why talk about other subjects?
replies: To show maths should not replace languages, humanities or arts.

answers.text: The text says the plan should not reduce opportunities in other areas, and that maths and the arts can enrich each other.
More maths shouldn't crowd out other important subjects.
I like that the author is balanced and not only pro-maths.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Pregunta nuestra sobre el Text N°4.

atoms.prompt: Do you agree that everyone should learn maths until 18? Why?
promptVariants: What is your opinion?
replies: Open answer: your own opinion.

answers.text: I agree, because maths helps in almost any career and in daily life.
In my opinion yes, but it should be interesting, not just more exams.
The text also says it develops skills for life, like logical thinking, so I agree.

atoms.speaker: examiner

source.note: Producción nuestra sobre el Text N°4.

atoms.prompt: Give a short presentation about the text on learning maths until 18.
atoms.modelAnswer: This text argues that everyone should study maths until age 18. First, maths opens doors to many careers, because it is the language of science, engineering and technology, and it includes statistics and Big Data. Second, maths helps in everyday life: we all make decisions about money, health and voting, and statistics are often used to persuade or even mislead us. Third, maths teaches transferable skills like problem-solving, logical thinking and healthy skepticism. The author compares learning maths to learning a language, which needs time and practice. She concludes that studying maths until 18 is a strong plan, if there are enough teachers.
scaffold: State the argument of the text.
Give the careers reason.
Give the everyday-life reason.
Give the skills reason.
End with the language comparison and conclusion.

rubric.text: Mencionás las habilidades o el escepticismo
Comparás la matemática con un idioma

Third, maths teaches transferable skills like problem-solving, logical thinking and healthy skepticism.
She compares learning maths to learning a language, and concludes that studying maths until 18 is a strong plan, if there are enough teachers.

Statistics are often used to support claims, and sometimes to mislead. Understanding ideas like averages, correlation and causation helps us make informed decisions. Maths also teaches us not to accept ideas without proof, which is a useful attitude in real life.

atoms.prompt: Do you agree that everyone should learn maths until 18? Give your opinion.
atoms.modelAnswer: In my opinion, learning maths until 18 is a good idea. Many people think maths is only useful for scientists, but the text shows that it helps everyone make better decisions and understand the world. It also teaches skills like logical thinking and skepticism. I do not think maths should replace other subjects, because arts and languages are important too, but a strong maths foundation helps in almost any career.

Say what many people think.
Say what the text shows.
Add a balanced point about other subjects.

source.note: Recurso de estudio: resumen fiel y atribuido del artículo, con citas breves. El artículo completo está en la fuente enlazada.

atoms.title: Should people learn maths until age 18?
atoms.speaker: narrator

blocks.text: This text argues that everyone should study maths until age 18. In 2023 the UK's prime minister announced a plan for all students in England to study maths until 18, which raises the question: why should everyone learn maths, even if not all jobs require it?

One reason is that maths opens doors to many careers. Maths is the language of science, engineering and technology, and a good foundation prepares students for any STEM subject. Maths also includes statistics and Big Data, which are essential in medicine, marketing, finance and online shopping.

Career opportunities are only part of the argument. Even people who do not use maths at work make decisions every day about what to buy, who to vote for and how to stay healthy. Statistics are often used to support claims, and sometimes to mislead. Understanding averages, correlation and causation helps us make informed decisions.

Maths also teaches transferable skills. Problem-solving and logical thinking are obvious ones, but maths also develops healthy skepticism: mathematicians do not accept ideas without proof, and this questioning attitude helps us evaluate claims made by politicians or advertisers.

Mathematical literacy is more than knowing formulas. Learning maths is similar to learning a language: knowing vocabulary and grammar is not enough, and you need to practice until it becomes part of your thinking. Once maths is embedded in your brain, you can recall it years later.

This plan should not reduce opportunities in other areas like languages, humanities or arts; in fact, maths and the arts can enrich each other. Keeping students engaged with maths until 18 widens career opportunities, helps people make informed decisions, and develops skills for life. The challenge now is to have enough teachers.

atoms.afterReadingTask: Find 5 examples of negative sentences in the Present Tense in the text.

source.note: Narrativa nuestra de comprension auditiva sobre el tema del texto.

atoms.narrative: Think about the surface of a ball, like the Earth. On a flat sheet of paper, the shortest path between two points is a straight line. But on a ball, the shortest path is a curve. Because of this, a triangle drawn on a ball can be strange: its three angles can add up to more than one hundred and eighty degrees.
atoms.speaker: narrator

questions.q: On a ball, is the shortest path a straight line or a curve?
accept: the shortest path is a curve

questions.q: Can the three angles of a triangle on a ball add up to more than 180 degrees?

source.note: Narrativa nuestra de comprension auditiva sobre el tema del texto.

atoms.narrative: Melanie loves the number two. She says it is small and simple, but very powerful. Two is the first prime number, and it creates the idea of even and odd numbers. For her, mathematics is not only about rules; it is also about being creative.
atoms.speaker: narrator

questions.q: According to her, does mathematics need creativity?
